title: Introduction to bmco
text: bmco package provides Bayesian methods analyzing multiple binary outcomes simultaneously. addresses common problem clinical trials, educational research, fields: compare groups success defined multiple endpoints.

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Suppose ’re evaluating new drug want know improves symptom relief quality life. bmco provides principled Bayesian framework : Analyzes multiple binary outcomes jointly Accounts correlations outcomes Offers flexible decision rules (, , weighted combinations) Provides posterior probabilities Estimates subgroup effects using data entire sample Handles clustered data

title: Introduction to bmco
text: posterior probability (pop) example tells us: “probability drug better placebo outcomes?”
code: library(bmco) set.seed(2024)  # Simulate a clinical trial with two binary outcomes trial_data <- data.frame(   treatment = rep(c("placebo", "drug"), each = 50),   symptom_relief = rbinom(100, 1, prob = rep(c(0.4, 0.6), each = 50)),   quality_of_life = rbinom(100, 1, prob = rep(c(0.5, 0.7), each = 50)) )  # Test if drug improves BOTH outcomes result <- bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   test = "right_sided",   rule = "All",   n_it = 1000 )  print(result) #>  #> Multivariate Bernoulli Analysis #> ================================= #>  #>    Group mean y1 mean y2 #>  placebo   0.520   0.481 #>     drug   0.616   0.639 #>   n(placebo) = 50    n(drug) = 50 #>  #> Posterior probability P(drug > placebo) [All rule]: 0.795 #>  #> Use summary() for credible intervals and ESS. summary(result) #>  #> Multivariate Bernoulli Analysis #> ================================= #>  #> Group Estimates: #>    Group mean y1 sd y1 mean y2 sd y2 #>  placebo   0.520 0.067   0.481 0.072 #>     drug   0.616 0.068   0.639 0.067 #>  #>   n(placebo) = 50    n(drug) = 50 #>  #> Treatment Effect: #>   Delta mean (y1, y2): 0.096, 0.157 #>   Delta SE   (y1, y2): 0.003, 0.003 #>   Posterior probability P(drug > placebo): 0.795 #>  #> Test Information: #>   Decision rule: All #>   Hypothesis: P(drug > placebo) #>  #>   (Run bmvb() with return_samples = TRUE for credible intervals and ESS.)

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Choose define “treatment success”:

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Treatment must improve outcomes:
code: result_all <- bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   rule = "All",  # BOTH outcomes must improve   n_it = 5000 )  cat("P(drug better on BOTH outcomes):", result_all$delta$pop, "\n") #> P(drug better on BOTH outcomes): 0.793

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Treatment must improve LEAST ONE outcome:
code: result_any <- bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   rule = "Any",  # At least one outcome must improve   n_it = 5000 )  cat("P(drug better on AT LEAST ONE outcome):", result_any$delta$pop, "\n") #> P(drug better on AT LEAST ONE outcome): 0.992

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Weight outcomes importance:
code: result_comp <- bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   rule = "Comp",   w = c(0.7, 0.3),  # Symptom relief weighted 70%, QoL 30%   n_it = 5000 )  cat("P(weighted improvement):", result_comp$delta$pop, "\n") #> P(weighted improvement): 0.936 cat("Weighted effect:", result_comp$delta$w_delta, "\n") #> Weighted effect: 0.114

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Test group B better group :
code: bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",         grp_b = "drug", # Put the group you expect to be better here   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   test = "right_sided",  # P(drug > placebo)   n_it = 5000 ) #>  #> Multivariate Bernoulli Analysis #> ================================= #>  #>    Group mean y1 mean y2 #>  placebo   0.519   0.481 #>     drug   0.617   0.635 #>   n(placebo) = 50    n(drug) = 50 #>  #> Posterior probability P(drug > placebo) [All rule]: 0.798 #>  #> Use summary() for credible intervals and ESS.

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Test group better group B: Note: Left-sided (, B) equivalent right-sided (B, ).
code: bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   test = "left_sided",  # P(placebo > drug)   n_it = 5000 ) #>  #> Multivariate Bernoulli Analysis #> ================================= #>  #>    Group mean y1 mean y2 #>  placebo   0.521   0.481 #>     drug   0.615   0.634 #>   n(placebo) = 50    n(drug) = 50 #>  #> Posterior probability P(placebo > drug) [All rule]: 0.012 #>  #> Use summary() for credible intervals and ESS.

code: result <- bmvb(   data = trial_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "placebo",   grp_b = "drug",   y_vars = c("symptom_relief", "quality_of_life"),   n_it = 5000 )  # Key components names(result) #> [1] "estimates"    "sample_sizes" "delta"        "info"  # Posterior estimates for each group result$estimates #> $mean_a #> [1] 0.520 0.481 #>  #> $mean_b #> [1] 0.615 0.635 #>  #> $sd_a #> [1] 0.069 0.069 #>  #> $sd_b #> [1] 0.066 0.066  # Sample sizes result$sample_sizes #> $n_a #> [1] 50 #>  #> $n_b #> [1] 50  # Treatment effect result$delta #> $mean_delta #> [1] 0.095 0.155 #>  #> $se_delta #> [1] 0.001 0.001 #>  #> $pop #> [1] 0.795  # Test information result$info #> $rule #> [1] "All" #>  #> $test #> [1] "right_sided" #>  #> $test_label #> [1] "P(drug > placebo)" #>  #> $grp_a #> [1] "placebo" #>  #> $grp_b #> [1] "drug"

title: Introduction to bmco
text: estimates$mean_a / mean_b: Posterior mean success probabilities group estimates$sd_a / sd_b: Posterior standard deviations delta$mean_delta: Mean treatment effect (difference probabilities) delta$se_delta: Standard error treatment effect delta$pop: Posterior probability (main result!) info$test_label: Human-readable description test

title: Introduction to bmco
text: Missing values automatically handled via complete-case analysis:

Rows with missing data will be #> removed.  # Sample sizes are reduced result_missing$sample_sizes #> $n_a #> [1] 45 #>  #> $n_b #> [1] 50

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
text: data clustered (e.g., students within schools, patients within hospitals), observations independent. Ignoring clustering can lead : Underestimated standard errors Inflated Type error rates Invalid inference - overpowered decisions bglmm() function extends bglm() handle clustered data incorporating random effects.

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
text: Use bglmm() : Observations grouped clusters (schools, hospitals, families, etc.); Cluster variation affects outcomes Clustering theoretically meaningful Use bglm() : Data clustered; Clustering theoretically meaningful

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
text: ’ll analyze study comparing two teaching methods across 20 schools reading math ability, differentiating student baseline ability.

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
code: library(bmco) set.seed(2020)  n_schools <- 20  n_per_school <- 15 n_total <- n_schools * n_per_school  # Generate random intercepts school_intercepts_math <- rnorm(n_schools, mean = 0, sd = 1.50) school_intercepts_reading <- rnorm(n_schools, mean = 0, sd = 1.50)  # Cluster randomization: whole schools receive the same method. school_method <- rep(c("traditional", "new_method"), each = n_schools/2)  study_data <- data.frame(   school_id = factor(rep(1:n_schools, each = n_per_school)),   method = rep(school_method, each = n_per_school),   baseline_ability = rnorm(n_total, mean = 50, sd = 10),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Standardize covariate study_data$baseline_ability_std <- scale(study_data$baseline_ability)[, 1]  study_data$math_pass <- NA study_data$reading_pass <- NA  p_math <- p_reading <- rep(NA, nrow(study_data))  for (i in 1:nrow(study_data)) {   school <- as.numeric(study_data$school_id[i])   is_new <- ifelse(study_data$method[i] == "new_method", 1, 0)    p_math[i]    <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.75 * is_new + 0.10 * study_data$baseline_ability_std[i] +                             0.20 * is_new * study_data$baseline_ability_std[i] +                             school_intercepts_math[school])   p_reading[i] <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.80 * is_new + 0.05 * study_data$baseline_ability_std[i] +                             0.15 * is_new * study_data$baseline_ability_std[i] +                             school_intercepts_reading[school])      study_data$math_pass[i]    <- rbinom(1, 1, p_math[i])   study_data$reading_pass[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p_reading[i]) }

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
code: set.seed(2025) result <- bglmm(   data = study_data,   grp = "method",   grp_a = "traditional",   grp_b = "new_method",   id_var = "school_id",   x_var = "baseline_ability",   y_vars = c("math_pass", "reading_pass"),   x_method = "Empirical",   x_def = c(-Inf, Inf),   fixed = c("method", "baseline_ability", "method_baseline_ability"),   random = c("Intercept"),   test = "right_sided",   rule = "All",   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500,   n_thin = 1 ) print(result) #>  #> Bayesian Multilevel Multivariate Logistic Regression #> code: nu0 <- p_random                # Degrees of freedom (dimension) tau0 <- diag(1e-1, p_random)   # Scale matrix

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes in Multilevel Data
text: Compare results different priors: Interpretation: results similar → data dominate, prior little influence results differ substantially → prior influential
code: # ----------------------------------------------------------------- # Three priors that pull in clearly different directions. # -----------------------------------------------------------------  # 1. Weakly informative (neutral starting point) #    Diffuse on fixed effects, diffuse IW for random effects. result_weak <- bglmm(   # ... other arguments ...   b_mu0    = rep(0, p_fixed),   b_sigma0 = solve(diag(10, p_fixed)),   g_mu0    = rep(0, p_random),   g_sigma0 = solve(diag(10, p_random)),   nu0  = p_random,   tau0 = diag(0.1, p_random), )  # 2. Skeptical prior #    Tight variance so the prior resists positive or negative evidence.  #    Also a small tau0, implying homogeneity between schools. result_skeptical <- bglmm(   # ... other arguments ...   b_mu0    = rep(0, p_fixed),                  b_sigma0 = solve(diag(c(1, 1), p_fixed)),  # Tight — hard(er) to overcome   g_mu0    = rep(0, p_random),   g_sigma0 = solve(diag(c(1, 1), p_random)), # Tight — hard(er) to overcome   nu0  = p_random + 4,                 # More informative IW   tau0 = diag(0.05, p_random),         # Expects small school variation )

text: function internally checks multivariate potential scale reduction factor (MPSRF) warns > 1.1. Additional MCMC diagnostics returned return_diagnostics = TRUE. return_diagnostic_plots = TRUE, bglmm() returns diagnostic plots well. Key diagnostics: MPSRF (Multivariate PSRF): Ideally < 1.1 Effective sample size: > 100 per parameter Rhat: Ideally < 1.1 parameters Available plots: Autocorrelation: ideally high lag 0, drops quickly toward 0 within lags. Traceplot: Chains fluctuate randomly around stable mean, clear trends, drifts, long flat stretches. Good mixing. Density: Posterior density individual parameters (regression coefficients variance parameters) preferably smooth, unimodal (unless theory suggests otherwise), without irregular spikes. convergence issues occur: Increase n_burn (warmup) Increase n_it (samples) Check clusters Check small cluster sizes Check range covariate consider standardizing Check problems warnings data (perfect separation, low variation, etc.) autocorrelation high, usually effective sample size much lower n_it: Consider thinning Consider increasing n_it information MCMC diagnostics, consult Brooks, S. P. & Gelman, . (1998). General methods monitoring convergence iterative simulations. Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 7(4), 434–455. Gelman, . & Rubin, D. B. (1992). Inference iterative simulation using multiple sequences. Statistical Science, 7(4), 457–472.

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
code: library(bmco) set.seed(2024)  n <- 150 clinical_data <- data.frame(   treatment = rep(c("standard", "new"), each = n/2),   age = rnorm(n, mean = 55, sd = 12) )  # Treatment is more effective in younger patients clinical_data$pain_relief <- NA clinical_data$mobility_improved <- NA  for (i in 1:nrow(clinical_data)) {   is_new <- ifelse(clinical_data$treatment[i] == "new", 1, 0)   age_centered <- (clinical_data$age[i] - 55) / 10      # Younger patients benefit more from new treatment (interaction effect)   p_pain <- plogis(-0.5 + 0.8 * is_new - 0.3 * age_centered - 0.4 * is_new * age_centered)   p_mobility <- plogis(-0.3 + 0.6 * is_new - 0.2 * age_centered - 0.3 * is_new * age_centered)      clinical_data$pain_relief[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p_pain)   clinical_data$mobility_improved[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p_mobility) }  # View data head(clinical_data) #>   treatment      age pain_relief mobility_improved #> 1  standard 66.78363           0                 1 #> 2  standard 60.62458           0                 1 #> 3  standard 53.70434           1                 0 #> 4  standard 52.44546           1                 1 #> 5  standard 68.89718           0                 1 #> 6  standard 70.50826           0                 0 summary(clinical_data$age) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>   15.71   46.48   54.80   54.43   63.16   81.50 table(clinical_data$treatment) #>  #>      new standard  #>       75       75

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
text: model includes: Intercept: Baseline log-odds reference group (standard treatment, mean age) Group effect: Main effect treatment Age effect: Effect age (centered) Interaction: treatment effect varies age multinomial regression coefficients straightforward interpretation terms treatment effects. Therefore, (posterior samples ) regression coefficients transformed (posterior samples ) marginal success probabilities (𝛉\mathbf{\theta}_{}, 𝛉B\mathbf{\theta}_{B}) marginal probability differences (𝛅=𝛉B−𝛉\mathbf{\delta} = \mathbf{\theta}_{B} - \mathbf{\theta}_{}) used analysis decision-making via three abovementioned methods.

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
text: three decision rules work:

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
code: result_all <- bglm(   data = clinical_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "standard",   grp_b = "new",   x_var = "age",   y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),   x_method = "Value",   x_def = 55,   rule = "All",  # Both outcomes must improve   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500 ) cat("Mean delta:", result_all$delta$mean_delta, "\n") #> Mean delta: 0.174 -0.001 cat("P(new treatment better on BOTH outcomes | age=55):", result_all$delta$pop, "\n") #> P(new treatment better on BOTH outcomes | age=55): 0.481

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
code: result_any <- bglm(   data = clinical_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "standard",   grp_b = "new",   x_var = "age",   y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),   x_method = "Value",   x_def = 55,   rule = "Any",  # At least one outcome must improve   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500 ) cat("Mean delta:", result_any$delta$mean_delta, "\n") #> Mean delta: 0.168 -0.006 cat("P(new treatment better on AT LEAST ONE outcome | age=55):", result_any$delta$pop, "\n") #> P(new treatment better on AT LEAST ONE outcome | age=55): 0.993

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
code:
result_comp <- bglm(   data = clinical_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "standard",   grp_b = "new",   x_var = "age",   y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),   x_method = "Value",   x_def = 55,   rule = "Comp",   w = c(0.6, 0.4),  # Pain relief weighted more heavily   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500 ) cat("Mean delta:", result_comp$delta$mean_delta, "
") #> Mean delta: 0.171 -0.008 cat("P(weighted improvement | age=55):", result_comp$delta$pop, "
") #> P(weighted improvement | age=55): 0.961 cat("Weighted effect:", result_comp$delta$w_delta, "
") #> Weighted effect: 0.1

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
text: Analyze treatment effects across age quartiles: reveals treatment effectiveness varies across age groups.
code: age_quartiles <- quantile(clinical_data$age, probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1))  subgroup_results <- data.frame(   age_group = c("Q1 (youngest)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (oldest)"),   age_range = paste0(     round(age_quartiles[1:4]), "-",      round(age_quartiles[2:5])   ),   mean_delta1 = NA,   mean_delta2 = NA,   post_prob = NA )  for (i in 1:4) {   result_q <- bglm(     data = clinical_data,     grp = "treatment",     grp_a = "standard",     grp_b = "new",     x_var = "age",     y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),     x_method = "Empirical",     x_def = c(age_quartiles[i], age_quartiles[i + 1]),     rule = "All",     n_burn = 1000,     n_it = 3000   )   subgroup_results$mean_delta1[i] <- result_q$delta$mean_delta[1]   subgroup_results$mean_delta2[i] <- result_q$delta$mean_delta[2]   subgroup_results$post_prob[i] <- result_q$delta$pop }  print(subgroup_results) #>       age_group age_range mean_delta1 mean_delta2 post_prob #> 1 Q1 (youngest)     16-46       0.209      -0.009     0.462 #> 2            Q2     46-55       0.195      -0.005     0.469 #> 3            Q3     55-63       0.150      -0.011     0.434 #> 4   Q4 (oldest)     63-82       0.080      -0.014     0.324

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
text: p number fixed effects: Intercept Group effect Covariate effect(s) Interaction term(s)
code: # Default for bglm/bglmm p <- 4 b_mu0 <- rep(0, p)                 # Zero prior mean b_sigma0 <- solve(diag(100, p))    # Small prior precision (large prior variance). Weakly informative.                                     # solve() generates precision matrix from variance matrix

title: Subgroup Analysis with Multivariate Binary Outcomes
text: Example 1: Informative prior favoring positive treatment effect Example 2: Skeptical prior (effect expected)
code: # Assume treatment improves outcomes (positive coefficient on group) # For binary covariate with 4 parameters: Intercept, grp, x, grp:x  custom_b_mu0 <- c(0, 0.5, 0, 0)      # Expect positive group effect custom_b_sigma0 <- solve(diag(c(10, 5, 10, 10)))  # More certainty on group effect result_informative <- bglm(   data = clinical_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "standard",   grp_b = "new",   x_var = "age",   y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),   x_method = "Value",   x_def = 55,   b_mu0 = custom_b_mu0,   b_sigma0 = custom_b_sigma0,   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500 ) # Skeptical about treatment effect - tight prior around zero skeptical_b_mu0 <- c(0, 0, 0, 0)        # No effect expected skeptical_b_sigma0 <- solve(diag(c(1, 1, 1, 1)))  # Small variance  result_skeptical <- bglm(   data = clinical_data,   grp = "treatment",   grp_a = "standard",   grp_b = "new",   x_var = "age",   y_vars = c("pain_relief", "mobility_improved"),   x_method = "Value",   x_def = 55,   b_mu0 = skeptical_b_mu0,   b_sigma0 = skeptical_b_sigma0,   n_burn = 200,   n_it = 500 )

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
text: Perform Bayesian test differences two (sub)groups multiple binary outcomes using multinomial logistic regression described Kavelaars et al. (2024) .

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
code: bglm(   data,   grp,   grp_a,   grp_b,   x_var,   y_vars,   x_method = c("Empirical", "Analytical", "Value"),   x_def = c(-Inf, Inf),   test = c("right_sided", "left_sided"),   rule = c("All", "Any", "Comp"),   w = NULL,   b_mu0 = NULL,   b_sigma0 = NULL,   n_burn = 10000,   n_it = 20000,   n_thin = 1,   n_chain = 2,   start = c(0.5, 1),   return_diagnostics = TRUE,   return_diagnostic_plots = FALSE,   return_samples = FALSE )

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
text: data Data frame containing data. grp Character string. Name grouping variable (treated factor). grp_a Value grp indicating first group. grp_b Value grp indicating second group. x_var Character string. Name covariate variable (currently supports single continuous binary covariate) y_vars Character vector. Names outcome variables (currently supports 2 outcomes). x_method Character. Method handling covariate: "Analytical" (numerical integration), "Empirical" (empirical marginalization), "Value" (specific value). Default "Empirical". x_def Numeric vector. Defines subpopulation: length-2 vector c(lower, upper) "Analytical"/"Empirical", scalar "Value". Default c(-Inf, Inf). test Character. Direction test: "left_sided" P(>B) "right_sided" P(B>). Default "right_sided". rule Character. Decision rule: "" (outcomes favor hypothesis), "" (least one outcome favors hypothesis), "Comp" (weighted combination). Default "". w Numeric vector. Weights compensatory rule. used rule = "Comp". NULL rule = "Comp", equal weights used. Default NULL. b_mu0 Vector prior means fixed regression coefficients. Default rep(0, P), P refers number columns model matrix. b_sigma0 Prior covariance matrix (PxP) regression coefficients. Default diag(1e-2, P), P refers number columns model matrix. n_burn Integer. Number burn-iterations. Default 10000. n_it Integer. Number MCMC iterations. Default 20000. n_thin Integer. Thinning interval. Default 1. n_chain Integer. Number MCMC chains sampled. Default 2. start Numeric vector. Starting values chains. length n_chain. Default c(0.5, 1). return_diagnostics Logical. Return MCMC diagnostics? Default TRUE. return_diagnostic_plots Logical. MCMC chains diagnostic plots (traceplots, autocorrelation, density) returned? Default FALSE. TRUE, diagnostics returned default. return_samples Logical. posterior samples returned? Default FALSE.

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
text: object class bglm, list containing: estimates list posterior means standard deviations group probabilities (mean_a, mean_b, sd_a, sd_b), well posterior means (b) standard deviations (b_sd) regression coefficients. sample_sizes list group sample sizes (n_a, n_b). delta list posterior mean differences (mean_delta), posterior standard errors (se_delta), posterior probability hypothesis (pop), , rule = "Comp", weighted difference (w_delta). info list prior specifications, test settings, group labels, covariate handling method, subpopulation definition. diags diagnostics requested, list MCMC diagnostic results regression coefficients. samples return_samples = TRUE, list containing posterior draws theta_a, theta_b, delta, regression coefficients.

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
text: Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2024). “Bayesian Multivariate Logistic Regression Superiority Inferiority Decision-Making Observable Treatment Heterogeneity.” Multivariate Behavioral Research, 59(4), 859–882. doi:10.1080/00273171.2024.2337340 .

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Model — bglm
code: # Example with simulated data # Generate data set.seed(123) n <- 100  data <- data.frame(  group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = n/2),  x = rnorm(n),  stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  p1 <- p2 <- rep(NA, n)  for (i in 1:n) {  grpB <- ifelse(data$group[i] == "B", 1, 0)   p1[i] <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.75 * grpB + 0.10 * data$x[i] + 0.20 * grpB * data$x[i])  p2[i] <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.80 * grpB + 0.05 * data$x[i] + 0.15 * grpB * data$x[i])   data$y1[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p1[i])  data$y2[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p2[i]) }  # Analyze result <- bglm(  data = data,  grp = "group",  grp_a = "A",  grp_b = "B",  x_var = "x",  y_vars = c("y1", "y2"),  x_method = "Empirical",  x_def = c(-Inf, Inf),  test = "right_sided",  rule = "All",  n_burn = 100, # Too low for proper MCMC sampling  n_it = 500 # Too low for proper MCMC sampling )  print(result) #>  #> Bayesian Multivariate Logistic Regression #> ========================================== #>  #>  Group mean y1 mean y2 #>      A   0.278   0.508 #>      B   0.637   0.559 #>   n(A) = 50    n(B) = 50 #>  #> Posterior probability P(B > A) [All rule]: 0.708 #> Marginalization: Empirical over [-Inf, Inf] #>  #> Use summary() for regression coefficients, priors and MCMC diagnostics. #>

text: data Data frame containing data. grp Character string. Method handling covariate. Default "Empirical". x_def Numeric. Defines subpopulation. Default c(-Inf, Inf). test Character. Direction test: "left_sided" P(>B) "right_sided" P(B>). title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Mixed Model — bglmm
text: Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2023). “Bayesian multilevel multivariate logistic regression superiority decision-making observable treatment heterogeneity.” BMC Medical Research Methodology, 23(1). doi:10.1186/s12874-023-02034-z .

title: Bayesian Generalized Linear Mixed Model — bglmm
code: # \donttest{ # Example with simulated data # Generate data set.seed(123) J <- 20 # No. clusters nJ <- 15 # Sample size per cluster  # Generate random intercepts uj_1 <- rnorm(J) uj_2 <- rnorm(J) data <- data.frame(  id = factor(rep(1:J, each = nJ)),  group = rep(rep(c("A", "B"), each = J/2), each = nJ),  x = rnorm(J * nJ),  stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  p1 <- p2 <- rep(NA, J * nJ)  for (i in 1:(J * nJ)) {  j <- as.numeric(data$id[i])  grpB <- ifelse(data$group[i] == "B", 1, 0)   p1[i] <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.75 * grpB + 0.10 * data$x[i] + 0.20 * grpB * data$x[i] + uj_1[j])  p2[i] <- plogis(-0.50 + 0.80 * grpB + 0.05 * data$x[i] + 0.15 * grpB * data$x[i] + uj_2[j])   data$y1[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p1[i])  data$y2[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, p2[i]) }  # Analyze result <- bglmm(  data = data,  grp = "group",  grp_a = "A",  grp_b = "B",  id_var = "id",  x_var = "x",  y_vars = c("y1", "y2"),  x_method = "Empirical",  x_def = c(-Inf, Inf),  fixed = c("group", "x", "group_x"),  random = c("Intercept"), # Random intercept model  test = "right_sided",  rule = "All",  n_burn = 100, # Too low for proper MCMC sampling  n_it = 500 # Too low for proper MCMC sampling  ) #> Warning: 20 cluster(s) have observations from only one group. This may affect estimation. #> Warning: Low effective sample size for some parameters. Results may be unreliable. #> Warning: Low effective sample size for some parameters. Results may be unreliable. #> Warning: Low effective sample size for some parameters. Results may be unreliable. #> Warning: MCMC chains may not have converged (MPSRF > 1.1). Consider increasing n_burn or n_it.  print(result) # Warnings due to low number of MCMC iterations (n_burn and n_it) #>  #> Bayesian Multilevel Multivariate Logistic Regression #> ====================================================== #>  #>  Group mean y1 mean y2 #>      A   0.448   0.341 #>      B   0.631   0.623 #>   J = 20 clusters    n(A) = 150    n(B) = 150 #>  #> Posterior probability P(B > A) [All rule]: 1.000 #> Marginalization: Empirical over [-Inf, Inf] #> MPSRF: fixed = 1.1516    random = 1.1371    variance = 1.0614 #>  #> Use summary() for full coefficient tables, priors and MCMC diagnostics. #>  # }

what: bmco: Bayesian Analysis for Multivariate Categorical Outcomes — bmco-package
title: bmco: Bayesian Analysis for Multivariate Categorical Outcomes — bmco-package
text: Provides Bayesian methods comparing groups multiple binary outcomes, including basic tests, regression adjustment, multilevel models.

title: bmco: Bayesian Analysis for Multivariate Categorical Outcomes — bmco-package
text: bmvb: Bayesian test using multivariate Bernoulli bglm: Subgroup analysis using Bayesian logistic regression analysis bglmm: Multilevel data using Bayesian multilevel logistic regression analysis

title: bmco: Bayesian Analysis for Multivariate Categorical Outcomes — bmco-package
text: Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2020). “Decision-making multiple correlated binary outcomes clinical trials.” Statistical Methods Medical Research, 29(11), 3265–3277. doi:10.1177/0962280220922256 .Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2024). “Bayesian Multivariate Logistic Regression Superiority Inferiority Decision-Making Observable Treatment Heterogeneity.” Multivariate Behavioral Research, 59(4), 859–882. doi:10.1080/00273171.2024.2337340 .Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2023). “Bayesian multilevel multivariate logistic regression superiority decision-making observable treatment heterogeneity.” BMC Medical Research Methodology, 23(1). doi:10.1186/s12874-023-02034-z .

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
text: Perform Bayesian test differences two groups multiple binary outcomes using Multivariate Bernoulli distribution, described Kavelaars et al. (2020) .

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
code: bmvb(   data,   grp,   grp_a,   grp_b,   y_vars,   test = c("right_sided", "left_sided"),   rule = c("All", "Any", "Comp"),   w = NULL,   prior_a = 0.5,   prior_b = 0.5,   n_it = 10000,   return_samples = FALSE )

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
text: data Data frame containing data. grp Character string. Name grouping variable. grp_a Value grp indicating first group. grp_b Value grp indicating second group. y_vars Character vector. Names outcome variables (currently supports 2 outcomes). test Character. Direction test: "left_sided" P(>B) "right_sided" P(B>). Default "right_sided". rule Character. Decision rule: "" (outcomes favor hypothesis), "" (least one outcome favors hypothesis), "Comp" (weighted combination). Default "". w Numeric vector. Weights compensatory rule. used rule = "Comp". NULL rule = "Comp", equal weights used. Default NULL. prior_a Numeric. Prior hyperparameter (Dirichlet) group . Default 0.5 (Jeffreys' prior) prior_b Numeric. Prior hyperparameter (Dirichlet) group B. Default 0.5 (Jeffreys' prior). n_it Integer. Number MCMC iterations. Default 10000. return_samples Logical. posterior samples returned? Default FALSE.

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
text: object class bmvb, list containing: estimates list posterior means (mean_a, mean_b) standard deviations (sd_a, sd_b) category probabilities groups. sample_sizes list group sample sizes (n_a, n_b). delta list posterior mean differences (mean_delta), posterior standard errors (se_delta), posterior probability hypothesis (pop), , rule = "Comp", weighted difference (w_delta). info list test specifications, including decision rule, test direction, group labels, weights (applicable). samples return_samples = TRUE, list containing posterior draws theta_a, theta_b, delta.

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
text: Kavelaars X, Mulder J, Kaptein M (2020). “Decision-making multiple correlated binary outcomes clinical trials.” Statistical Methods Medical Research, 29(11), 3265–3277. doi:10.1177/0962280220922256 .

title: Bayesian Multivariate Bernoulli Test — bmvb
code: # \donttest{ # Example with simulated data # Generate data set.seed(123) data <- data.frame( treatment = rep(c("control", "drug"), each = 50),  outcome1 = rbinom(100, 1, 0.5),  outcome2 = rbinom(100, 1, 0.5) )  # Analyze result <- bmvb(  data = data,  grp = "treatment",  grp_a = "control",  grp_b = "drug",  y_vars = c("outcome1", "outcome2"),  n_it = 10000 )  print(result) #>  #> Multivariate Bernoulli Analysis #> ================================= #>  #>    Group mean y1 mean y2 #>  control   0.501   0.501 #>     drug   0.442   0.501 #>   n(control) = 50    n(drug) = 50 #>  #> Posterior probability P(drug > control) [All rule]: 0.129 #>  #> Use summary() for credible intervals and ESS. #>  # }
